name: SoFi Technologies, Inc.
headline: A real digital bank charter with a tech-platform business attached — and the market still treats it like a lender.

children.text: SoFi's bank charter unlocked deposit-funded lending and let it stop renting other banks' balance sheets. The under-appreciated piece is the Technology Platform (Galileo + Technisys) — that's effectively 'Stripe for banks-as-a-service' running at software gross margins.

Member growth is still compounding above 30% annually with rising cross-buy across products. The mix shift away from low-margin lending into higher-margin financial services and tech platform is the story the market is slowly catching up to.

bullCase: Deposit-funded model materially improves NIM versus warehouse-funded competitors.
Galileo / Technisys tech platform is a true software business hidden inside a bank.
Member growth still 30%+ with cross-buy of products rising every cohort.
Path to GAAP profitability has materialized faster than skeptics priced.
Optionality if interchange and crypto regulations stabilize.
bearCase: Credit cycle: personal loan losses tick up if employment softens.
Fintech competition (Robinhood, Cash App, Chime) is intense at the front of the funnel.
Stock-based compensation still high relative to GAAP earnings.
Interest rate sensitivity cuts both ways on NIM.
Regulatory risk on crypto and on student loan refi normalization.

name: Rivian Automotive, Inc.
headline: The R2 launch + the VW partnership reframed Rivian from cash burn story to scaling EV platform.

children.text: Rivian's gross margin trajectory is what changed our minds. The cost-down work on R1 plus the architecture redesign for R2 means each vehicle is now contributing meaningfully more cash. The VW joint venture takes the second-biggest worry — software investment — and turns it into a multi-billion-dollar funding source.
This is still a high-risk small-cap-by-market-cap story stock. But the runway and the technical execution are categorically better than where the stock is priced.

bullCase: R2 platform addresses the volume tier where EV adoption actually scales.
VW partnership funds software stack and validates Rivian's tech.
Gross margin turning positive ahead of consensus expectations.
Brand and product quality remain industry-leading in their segments.
Commercial van program (Amazon and beyond) gives a steady revenue base.
bearCase: Cash burn still substantial; needs continued capital discipline.
EV demand has cooled — affordable segment is brutally competitive.
Tax credit and policy risk under shifting US administration agendas.
Tesla pricing pressure and Chinese OEM cost structure are real threats.
Highly dilutive history; share count keeps creeping up.

catalysts.label: Quarterly delivery and gross margin prints

name: BlackBerry Limited
headline: A patient turnaround: QNX in every connected vehicle, cybersecurity stabilizing, and a sum-of-parts gap.

children.text: BlackBerry trades for less than the implied value of its QNX automotive embedded business alone — and that business has design wins in roughly 235 million vehicles, with auto OEMs continuing to pick QNX as the foundational layer for software-defined vehicles.

The cybersecurity segment has been a drag for years but appears to be stabilizing. Management has been consistent about separating the two businesses to surface value. The asymmetric setup is real but the timing is unknowable.

bullCase: QNX is the dominant safety-certified embedded OS in the SDV transition.
Sum-of-parts analysis suggests significant value above current price.
Cybersecurity revenue declines have flattened — stabilization in sight.
Royalty backlog provides multi-year revenue visibility.
Activist and strategic interest in QNX separation has surfaced repeatedly.
bearCase: Cybersecurity has been a value trap for the better part of a decade.
Royalty revenue is back-end loaded — recognition can be lumpy.
Heavy Canadian regulatory and currency exposure for US-reporting investors.
If a separation doesn't materialize, the stock can stagnate.
Small float and elevated short interest can swing both ways.

name: IonQ, Inc.
headline: A pure-play bet on whether quantum computing has its 'AlexNet moment' in the next 36 months.

children.text: IonQ uses trapped-ion architecture, which has higher fidelity per qubit than superconducting approaches at the cost of gate speed. They've shipped systems to AWS, Azure, and major research labs, and have meaningful US government contract revenue. The question is whether 'useful quantum advantage' arrives this decade.

This is purely a bet on the technology arriving. Don't size it like a normal stock. We treat IonQ as a small option position — high asymmetric upside if quantum-AI hybrid workflows become real, near-zero if not.

bullCase: Trapped-ion approach has the highest demonstrated fidelity per qubit.
Cloud distribution via AWS Braket, Azure Quantum, GCP gives instant developer reach.
Federal contracts (DOE, AFRL) provide validating, recurring revenue.
Acquisitions (Qubitekk, Lightsynq) build out the ecosystem.
Massive optionality if quantum-AI hybrid workloads become real.
bearCase: Real commercial quantum advantage is unproven at scale.
Cash burn is heavy and dilutive secondaries are likely.
Competitive set (Google, IBM, PsiQuantum, Quantinuum) is technically formidable and better-resourced.
Stock trades like a meme at times — high volatility unrelated to fundamentals.
If the technology disappoints, drawdown could be 70-90%.

name: WELL Health Technologies Corp.
headline: Canada's largest owner-operator of primary care clinics — and a stealth software business growing inside it.

children.text: WELL has rolled up Canadian and US primary care clinics while building electronic medical record software (OSCAR Pro, Cerner conversions) used by thousands of practitioners. The clinic side throws off cash; the software side is the high-margin compounder hidden underneath.

Recent moves to spin out non-core US assets and focus the parent on Canada + AI-augmented practitioner workflows are the kind of capital allocation discipline this story needed. Still small, still volatile — but the operating fundamentals are improving.

bullCase: Largest practitioner-led primary care network in Canada.
EMR / clinic software is a high-margin recurring layer with switching costs.
AI-assisted documentation and workflow products are real product, not vapor.
M&A roll-up playbook has executed reasonably well at small scale.
Strategic moves to focus on Canada + spin out US assets clean up the story.
bearCase: Market cap is small — institutional ownership is thin and volatility is high.
M&A integration risk is constant given pace of acquisitions.
Canadian healthcare reimbursement changes can move earnings sharply.
High debt load from acquisitions limits flexibility.
US practitioner business has been a margin drag and capital allocation distraction.
